title: Most difficult thing about Bike Touring   Request

enrichment.summary: Kyle responds to a viewer request about the hardest challenges of bike touring through Europe. He identifies motivation to camp instead of paying for hotels, eating cheaply on canned food, and navigating roads without GPS as the biggest difficulties, while dismissing wind, elevation, and cold weather as non-issues with proper gear.
enrichment.speaker: kyle

stances.position: Motivating yourself to camp in bushes instead of paying for a hotel, especially in rain
Eating canned food is the right call — worth traveling longer on cheap food than shorter on nice meals
Worth the expense every time — those are the memories that matter
Not a real issue if you have proper gear
Never a real problem — someone always speaks English

stances.topic: Muslim women who refused to give directions
stances.position: Strongly negative — expressed hostility toward immigrants who don't assimilate
Dangerous drivers; passing too close at high speed
Go as light as possible; early tours he was too heavy

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The hardest challenge bicycling through Europe is motivating to sleep in the bushes instead of getting a hotel.

claims.subject: Europe
claims.claim: Europe is really populated, like the East Coast on steroids with houses everywhere.

claims.subject: Europe
claims.claim: Camping is illegal in Europe, so it's hard to find a hiding place to sleep.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: The hardest part is motivating yourself to camp in the bushes when it's raining.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Living on cheap food instead of expensive meals is hard but allows you to travel longer.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Eating cheaply involves downing cans of vegetables, chili, and other cheap canned foods.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Wind, elevation, and cold weather are not an issue if you have the proper gear.

claims.subject: Poland
claims.claim: Poland has very dangerous drivers who pass closely at high speeds.

claims.subject: Mexico
claims.claim: Mexico has bad roads with no shoulders, making biking dangerous.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Finding small roads instead of super highways is difficult when biking through towns.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Navigating through towns and ensuring you take the right road out is time-consuming.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Language barriers aren't a problem because someone always speaks English.

claims.subject: Asia
claims.claim: In Asia, women often refuse to give directions and defer to men.

claims.subject: Muslim women
claims.claim: Muslim women with veils sometimes ignore questions and walk away.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: If he were to bike tour again, he would be more motivated to avoid hotels and eat canned food.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: Drinking and partying while traveling is worth the expense because of the memories.

claims.subject: Kyle
claims.claim: He plans to make more biking videos, especially around Boulder, Colorado.

transcript.text: Okay, doing video requests. All these people giving me video requests.
I didn't even see this, and it was six days ago. Sorry if it took me so long. I didn't even notice it was yonder.
Okay, next video request from J-F-L-A-N-D-R-V-O-L.
I know him. That's James.
Okay, next video request.
Is there any way you can do a tonight video request on the hardest challenges bicycle touring in Europe?
Was it the wind, the elevation, cold weather, heavy gear, language barrier, or what?
What would you change?
I'm still really into biking videos from when you were touring.
I think that was awesome. Thanks, Matt.
Okay, oh, Matt.
Okay, so what was the hardest thing?
The hardest challenge bicycling touring through Europe?
I think the hardest challenge bicycling touring through Europe is motivating to sleep in the bushes instead of getting a hotel.
There are a few times where, see, in Europe, Europe is really populated.
It's kind of like the East Coast on steroids, I guess.
I don't know. It's like just fucking houses everywhere, dude.
And so, and it's also illegal to camp.
And so, it wasn't my idea to get busted camping.
And so, but I remember one time, you know, and I probably could have hid somewhere, but I got a hotel.
And then another time I got a hotel because it was raining.
And so, the hardest challenge is to motivate yourself to camp in the bushes when it's raining, you know,
Or when you're in a super populated place and you can't find a hiding place.
That will save you like 65 bucks a night.
That was the hardest part.
Also, living on cheap food is hard.
Instead of going and having more expensive meals, nice pizzas and expensive meals are good and healthy and all that.
But you can save so much money and you can travel for so much longer if you eat the way I do.
I mean, I learned how to eat cheaply from living in my house.
And if I were to go bike touring now, oh my God, I could do it really cheap.
If I were to do it again, I were, you know, the best way to do it if you want to, you know, do it cheaply is to just canned food, man.
Just open up canned food, down it, you know, spoon right out of the can, throw it in the trash, right there on the freaking sidewalk.
Down a bunch of cans of like vegetables and like chili and shit.
And then throw it in the side and then maybe put another cans and just eat cans, like $1.50 cans of shit.
So cheap instead of going to restaurants.
So that's the other challenge.
Okay, wind, elevation, and cold weather.
Wind, elevation, and cold weather are not an issue if you have the proper gear on, if you have the proper gloves and hats and stuff.
So that's not a problem at all.
I guess sometimes you can get a little bit worried that you're going to get hit by a car if you're out there in the rain because they may not see you.
In some countries, they are very dangerous drivers, like Poland, for example, are unbelievable.
You know, like they'll pass you like one inch to spare and pass each other at 100 miles an hour and it's just, Mexico's really bad like that.
Also because the roads are really bad.
If the roads, there's no, if there's no shoulder on the road, like I was in Mexico and there was no shoulder and there were these huge REITs.
And so I couldn't move over and there was no shoulder and there was traffic going 60 miles an hour.
It scared the fucking shit out of me.
That is the worst part right there.
Okay.
Also finding roads, finding little roads.
You go up to a place, you're trying to find a city.
All you see is the fucking superhighway and you're like, where the hell is the little road and where did it go and which one is it?
That's a little bit difficult.

And like getting into a town and then being able to roll through the town and being able to pop out of the town in the right direction to the next new town is the most difficult time consuming problem.
Because you always have to ask people to make sure that you're leaving the right city, the right road.
Because I didn't have like GPS when I was there.
So, because if you leave the wrong road in one of these small little towns, you could go to the wrong place and it could cost some time.
I mean, you know, you could get out of the way.
Okay.
So, cold weather, heavy gear.
Yes.
Going really light.
Like the very first times I biked here, I guess I was too heavy.
I want to go really light.
If you look at how I biked here the last time in 2010, that's the best way to go.
Super, super light.
Language barrier.
There's never a language barrier.
Because if somebody doesn't speak English, you just ask somebody else and somebody else will speak English.
No matter where you are, there's always somebody going to be speaking English.
So, the language barrier is not a problem.
Although, getting directions can be a problem if you're in Asia.
Because women don't give directions.
They always try to get their man to give you a direction, even though he's like a fucking half mile away or a quarter mile away.
Ethnic women in Europe don't give directions, like the Muslims with their veils and stuff.
If you, once I asked a lady, and she didn't even, of one of those veiled Muslims in the Netherlands.
All I said was, is this the trail, is this the track that goes to the next town?
And she just turned around for me and walked the other direction.
Didn't say anything.
Just turned around and walked the other direction.
That would creep the fuck out of me if I was like a European and there were some of these crazy Muslims around.
If you say, oh, hi, and they just turn around and walk the other direction.
It's like, why the fuck did you come to my fucking country, asshole?
If you're going to be like that.
You're supposed to come here to become fucking like us, dude.
If you're going to walk the other direction and have a fucking veil over your face, stay in bumfuck hellhole where you come from, man.
Anyway, sorry.
Got a little emotional there.
Women, dude.
Women.
See, because women from those countries are unbelievable.
I experience that kind of stuff in Southeast Asia a lot, too.
Ask questions and they just...
Once I asked my wife what direction north was and she got angry with me.
Why the fuck would somebody want to know which direction north is?
Sorry.
Okay.
Anyway, so what would you change?
I would be more motivated to not stay in a hotel and to get canned food because, oh, hell, I could have been traveling for a year, dude.
And it's way better to travel for a year on canned food than for six months on pizza and beer.
Fuck yeah.
Although I did drink a lot of beer and I did party a lot and that was fun.
But drinking and partying is not something you're going to regret even if it's expensive because those are the memories you're going to have.

That's always, always, always worth it.
Okay.
Is it really into your biking videos?
Oh, yeah.
You really...
Well, I'm going to do more biking videos.
I'm going to do biking videos all over Colorado.
I'm going to do biking videos.

All over Boulder.
So hopefully this summer.
So stay tuned.
All right.
Peace out.
